evidence.note: 100 rps / 200 msg/s target and 150ms latency budget carried forward into scalability.a and observability.a findings below

reviewed all 10 Link columns — consistent camelCase naming matching entity field names exactly; no naming-convention shift or near-duplicate column cluster; tag (added later) follows the same convention

checks.reason: Single-table schema with zero related tables — grep for FOREIGN KEY/REFERENCES across both DDL files found none. There is no foreign-key relationship to have gotten right or wrong yet; data-model.md explicitly defers the FK-vs-app-level-convention decision to when a second table is added.
No monetary/currency fields exist anywhere in this schema (data-model.md line 18, confirmed against all 10 Link columns) — there is no decimal-vs-float/currency type-choice decision to evaluate.

evidence.note: the only concretely-named next feature (link-shared events) is a new consumer, not a new entity/table, so current Link schema has no specific forward requirement to anticipate yet
all columns are scalar/typed (BIGINT, DATE, VARCHAR, DATETIME) — no JSON/TEXT blob column duplicating another entity's structured data

AnalyticsClient's timeout/circuit-breaker thresholds are literal (not ${VAR}-templated), but they sit on the analytics-forwarding path, which technical-vision.md explicitly states carries 'no hard SLA' — a literal value there doesn't threaten the one path (GET resolve) that does carry a stated latency budget

sole external call in the repo (per its own javadoc: 'The only outbound call this service makes to another system') has timeout+circuit-breaker guardrails; no second external call exists in-repo to compare it against for a consistency gap

checks.reason: The whole codebase has exactly one instance of every component type (1 REST resource, 1 consumer, 1 scheduled job, 1 external client, 1 entity) — there is nothing structurally analogous elsewhere to compare for a same-shape-modeled-differently inconsistency.

No code in this repository consumes or reprocesses messages from click-events-dlq — the DLQ failure-recovery path's mechanism (or lack thereof) is assessed as a recovery-mechanism gap under reliability-resilience.e; restating the same absence here as a distinct observability finding would duplicate that finding rather than surface a new fact.
extensibility.a found 1/1 adherence to the consumer pattern doc (a single real instance). With no second instance yet, there is no inconsistency whose ongoing replication cost could be measured.

click-events' message payload is a bare String (the short code), not a structured/versioned schema — there is no 'tolerates unknown fields' question for a scalar payload. The only other planned channel (link-shared) doesn't exist in code yet to check either.

No PII/financial fields exist anywhere in the schema (data-model.md, confirmed against DDL) and no logging statements exist anywhere in this codebase (grepped for Logger/LOG./log./System.out/println — zero matches) — there is nothing sensitive and no log output to audit for redaction.

evidence.note: create request body is @Valid with @NotBlank + @Pattern(regexp="^https?://.+") on targetUrl

resolve()'s @PathParam("code") has no explicit format/length constraint, unlike create's body — but this is a read-only, 404-safe lookup key (VARCHAR(12) column) with no evidenced injection vector; LinkRepository's actual query mechanism is an unwired stub, so downstream usage of the raw string couldn't be verified either way. Not elevated to a risk finding: the asymmetry is real but the concrete, checkable harm is not.

checks.reason: The entire HTTP surface is 2 endpoints (create, read-only resolve) — neither destructive/bulk/admin. The one bulk state-changing operation (LinkExpiryJob's expire sweep) is an automated internal scheduled job, not a user-triggered privileged action, so there is no privileged-operation gating decision to evaluate.
